title: Today's AI Brief: Norse Atlantic Airways Offers Dirt-Cheap Tickets. There’s a Catch
summary: A look at AI-driven scams in flight deals and South Africa's AI policy landscape.
imageAlt: Image from source article for Norse Atlantic Airways Offers Dirt-Cheap Tickets. There’s a Catch
imageCaption: Norse Atlantic Airways Offers Dirt-Cheap Tickets. There’s a Catch
imageSourceTitle: Norse Atlantic Airways Offers Dirt-Cheap Tickets. There’s a Catch

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to your AI Brief. Today, I'm looking at a couple of interesting stories that caught my eye in the world of artificial intelligence.

First up, it seems Norse Atlantic Airways has been making waves with dirt-cheap tickets, but there's a catch. Apparently, some folks received emails saying their flights were canceled, and they had just 14 days to request a refund. Sounds like a classic scam, right? But here's the twist: the airline claims to be using AI to combat these very scams. My read is that they're trying to stay one step ahead, but it's a cat-and-mouse game.

Next, I came across an article about South Africa's draft AI policy. The country has a lot of leverage in the AI space, but it seems their policy leaves a lot to be desired. What stands out to me is that they're not really utilizing their AI capabilities to their full potential. It's like having a superpower, but not quite knowing how to use it.

These stories might seem unrelated at first, but they both highlight the complexities of AI adoption. Whether it's using AI to prevent scams or creating policies that actually work, it's clear that we're still figuring things out. And that's what makes this space so fascinating – it's a constantly evolving landscape.

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.
